about_text.value: A cartographer is murdered inside a locked treaty chamber where testimony edits reality. A diplomat wants the death translated into policy, a magician wants it elevated into legend, and the corpse keeps suggesting the real victim was consensus itself. You are the last witness trusted by every liar. Choose the story the city will be forced to inhabit.

text_script.value: The dead cartographer lies under a table of mirrored border-stones, but his outline shifts whenever anyone speaks with confidence. Mirelle has already arranged the murder into a diplomatic problem. Vesper has already arranged it into a performance. The corpse, inconveniently, may still be listening. Each fresh accusation redraws the windows, as though testimony were redrafting the nation outside in real time.
Take Mirelle's strategic reconstruction seriously.

Mirelle narrates the summit as a board position disguised as grief. By the time she finishes, the murder looks less like rage than a pre-emptive alliance move. Vesper notices how much of her logic you are willing to borrow.
Mirelle redraws motive as an alliance preemption. Every pause she leaves becomes a place where guilt can be converted into necessity, and necessity starts sounding almost humane.
She inventories loyalties the way other people inventory wounds. By the end, the corpse seems less slain than reallocated, and the chamber briefly mistakes strategy for truth.
Let Vesper frame the murder as deliberate theater.

Vesper says locked rooms are only stages with stricter acoustics. He does not ask who killed the cartographer. He asks who benefits from making every witness remember a different disappearance. Mirelle files your attention to this answer under future risk.
Vesper treats contradiction like stage lighting: the brighter the inconsistency, the more persuaded the room becomes that a director exists somewhere behind it.
He turns the locked chamber into an audience chamber. What should have been evidence starts behaving like applause, and even Mirelle hesitates before calling that irrational.

The corpse opens one eye only after the living have nearly finished weaponizing him. His voice comes from the map pinned beneath him. He says the chamber is less interested in murder than in deciding whether the dead remain evidence, office, or infrastructure. Even the air waits like a clerk holding contradictory decrees and begging reality to sign only one of them.
Ask what the room wants instead of who swung the blade.

The corpse seems relieved. He says the room wants a story strong enough to keep the city from fragmenting into rival afterlives. Mirelle hears constitutional danger. Vesper hears artistic opportunity. You hear the stakes becoming cosmological.
The corpse says institutions are only graves that learned procedure. If the chamber wants a story, it wants one sturdy enough to keep metaphysics from seceding.
He speaks as if architecture itself voted for the murder. The border-stones hum in agreement, and the city beyond the walls starts feeling like a treaty written on fog.
Demand sequence, motive, angle, and instrument like this were still an earthly room.
The corpse answers with unnerving precision. Some of it is useful, some impossible, all of it clarifying. The room briefly remembers that fact can still wound myth.
You insist on instrument, angle, chronology. The corpse obliges so exactly that the room recoils from its own appetite for symbolism and remembers that facts can still cut.
Each concrete detail lands like a nail through a myth. Vesper smiles anyway, because even demolition can be theatrical when the witness is stubborn enough.

There are five shadows around the table and only four bodies willing to be counted. Mirelle calls the missing seat a sponsor. Vesper calls it an audience. The corpse calls it a role accepted whenever the room becomes frightened of ambiguity. The fifth shadow keeps adjusting its posture to match whoever sounds most certain, which is another way of saying the room is auditioning authorship.
Name the missing seat as the mastermind and stabilize the city around the legend.

The room relaxes the moment you provide an absent author. Cities love invisible culprits because invisible culprits excuse everything. Vesper becomes unreadable. Mirelle becomes interested. The corpse shuts his eyes like a man recognizing an efficient old sin.
The invisible architect settles over the chamber like incense. Everyone can breathe again now that agency has been exported to a legend with no address and infinite utility.
Naming an absent mastermind feels almost charitable. It spares the living from admitting that collective convenience might be more murderous than any single hand.
Reject the hidden-hand story and accuse the chamber itself of manufacturing culprits.

The room shudders as if contradicted in its mother tongue. Mirelle sees administrative danger in your answer. Vesper sees an insult to narrative craft. The corpse, for the first time, looks almost grateful.
You accuse the chamber of manufacturing culprits the way republics manufacture flags. For one dangerous second, nobody can tell whether you are solving the murder or indicting causality.
The answer offends everyone because it leaves no one cleanly outside the machine. Even the corpse looks less dead than vindicated by your refusal to flatter the room.

encounters.title: Choose the Story That Will Rule

text_script.value: The room no longer cares what happened. It cares which account will become architecture. Whatever you choose will decide whether the city keeps its borders, its legends, its dead, or its right to remain contradictory. The mirrored stones have stopped reflecting faces and now reflect only the futures your answer would make governable.
Let Mirelle annex the verdict into policy.
You choose the version that can survive in archives, armies, and ministries.
Mirelle receives your verdict with the gratitude of a state that has just inherited a usable ghost. The chamber hardens around paperwork, seals, and disciplined forgetting.
You let policy win because policy can survive being disbelieved. The blood becomes precedent before it can become scandal, and the room rewards the efficiency.
Let Vesper keep the legend and govern through dread.
You choose the story that does not need to be true as long as it remains unforgettable.
Vesper accepts the crown of explanation without ever claiming innocence. The city chooses dread over clarity because dread at least knows how to organize a crowd.
The verdict leaves truth unemployed and style sovereign. Rumor acquires civil-service powers the instant you decide unforgettable matters more than verifiable.
Return the cartographer to office and let the dead become the treaty.
You choose a verdict in which the dead are reinstated rather than interpreted.
The corpse rises into office not as miracle but as administrative continuity. Borders quiet down because even death cannot outvote a mapmaker with resumed authority.
You ratify the dead as a standing institution. The chamber approves because resurrection is easier to regulate than contradiction.
Admit that the missing seat belongs to the witness who kept arranging gravity.
You choose the possibility every other witness secretly needed.
Once spoken, the theory of you as hidden fifth seat begins arranging every prior silence into choreography. Suspicion becomes a kind of coronation.
You accept authorship the way some people accept exile. The room relaxes because at last the mystery has a face capable of surviving projection.
Distribute blame and force the city to keep contradiction visible.
You choose a verdict mature enough to leave tension intact.
No single culprit can absorb what this room required. Your verdict distributes the stain across alliance, vanity, fear, and architecture, and the city learns to live with plural guilt.
You deny the chamber its favorite narcotic: one answer, one monster, one absolution. In return it grants you the harder peace of unresolved intelligence.
Refuse a single culprit and step outside the chamber's appetite.
You choose the answer the room was built to suppress.
Instead of solving the room, you walk beyond the grammar that made solving it necessary. The walls thin, not because they agree with you, but because they can no longer contain the argument.
The chamber was built to convert terror into culprit. You refuse the conversion, and reality briefly remembers it is larger than jurisprudence.
Let the city forget rather than canonize the wrong truth.
You choose erasure over false closure.
Amnesia enters like a cheap treaty and everyone signs because forgetting costs less than metaphysical honesty. The city survives thinner, poorer, and eerily easier to govern.
You choose civic anesthesia. The room approves with the shamefaced relief of a regime that would rather misremember forever than learn what its fear was for.

encounters.title: Ending: The Ministry of Plausible Blood

text_script.value: Mirelle turns the murder into policy before the blood can cool. The city survives inside a disciplined lie. Foreign powers accept it because it is legible, useful, and easier than admitting reality briefly became negotiable.

encounters.title: Ending: The Legend Eats the State

text_script.value: Vesper curates fear of the unseen hand until rumor outranks ministry. The absent mastermind becomes doctrine. The city endures, but only by kneeling to an explanation designed to stay just out of reach.

encounters.title: Ending: The Dead Cartographer Returns to Office

text_script.value: The chamber accepts the dead as constitutional rather than incidental. The cartographer rises already revising borders. Nobody wins exactly, but the maps stop bleeding and diplomacy continues under a man who now treats mortality as a clerical inconvenience.

encounters.title: Ending: The Witness Was the Fifth Seat

text_script.value: The room rewards its favorite explanation: the quiet witness as hidden author. You do not need to have wielded the knife. It is enough that everyone can now imagine you teaching gravity how to lean.

You force the city to live with distributed blame and contradictory truth. It is ugly, unheroic, and more honest than the chamber wanted. The city survives by becoming intelligent enough to remain unresolved.

encounters.title: Ending: The City of Amnestied Memory

text_script.value: The murder is not solved so much as erased. The city still stands, but it can no longer explain the shape of its own fear. Peace arrives thin, cheap, and haunted.
You refuse to compress the event into a single culprit, and the chamber finally opens. The city survives because you reject the terms under which it was asked to understand itself.
